data.title: Microsoft joins consortium to build India-Southeast Asia undersea AI cable - report
data.description:
Microsoft (MSFT) has joined a consortium led by Singapore-based telecom startup Lightstorm to build a 3,600-km undersea cable linking India with Malaysia and Singapore.

The consortium, which also includes Tata Communications, Singapore Telecommunications (SGAPY), ASEAN Cableship and Japan's NEC (NECPY), will develop the I-2SEA cable system to support AI, cloud and hyperscale workloads, a Reuters report added.

The 3,600-km cable network will feature landing stations in Machilipatnam, Andhra Pradesh, where Meta (META) and Alphabet (GOOGL) have announced data center projects.

Lightstorm Group CEO and Managing Director Amajit Gupta said that the I-2SEA cable is expected to enter service in the fourth quarter of 2029, according to the report.

Gupta said that the I Squared-backed company currently connects 19 AI and cloud zones across India through its terrestrial fiber network, with the new cable expected to expand that footprint to 29 zones.

He added that Lightstorm plans to launch an IPO in India in mid-2027 and was reportedly seeking a valuation of up to $1.5B in March.

data.description:
• General Mills (GIS) declares $0.61/share quarterly dividend, in line with previous.
  • Forward yield 6.46%

data.title: AstraZeneca, Abbisko partner on lung cancer combination therapy trial
data.description:
Abbisko Therapeutics has entered into a strategic collaboration agreement with AstraZeneca (AZN) to jointly advance the clinical development of a novel IO-TKI combination therapy for non-small cell lung cancer, the companies said on Wednesday.

As a multicenter, open-label Phase I/II clinical study, the combination will evaluate the safety and efficacy of Abbisko's first-in-class oral small-molecule PD-L1 inhibitor, lumipodlin, in combination with AstraZeneca's third-generation EGFR-TKI, TAGRISSO (osimertinib), for the treatment of patients with EGFR-mutated and PD-L1 positive locally advanced or metastatic NSCLC.

On May 20, the investigational new drug application for the combination study was cleared by the National Medical Products Administration.

This Phase II study will be led by Abbisko, and both Abbisko and AstraZeneca will share responsibilities for the clinical trial.

data.title: OpenAI proposes giving Trump administration 5% equity stake: FT
data.description:
OpenAI (OPENAI) has discussed giving a 5% stake to the U.S. government as the $852B AI start-up seeks to clear political obstacles by securing financial buy-in from the Trump administration, the Financial Times reported on Thursday.

Sam Altman, chief executive of the ChatGPT maker, has argued that giving the public a financial stake in the company is the best way to share the upside of AI and has suggested a stake of this size in early conversations with the administration, according to two people familiar with the talks.

Altman has been in active talks with the administration about the issue of public ownership, including Trump, Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick, and Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, according to multiple people familiar with the matter.

The proposed arrangement would involve other US AI companies handing over a similar stake, although it is not clear if the other labs would be willing to do so, the report said.

OpenAI and its chief rival Anthropic (ANTHRO) have recently both had the release of their cutting-edge models held up by U.S. scrutiny, while some Republicans and advisers to President Donald Trump favour much tighter regulation of the sector.

data.title: Nvidia strikes revenue-sharing deals with Sharon AI, cloud partners to scale AI factories
data.description:
• Nvidia (NVDA) Tuesday said that it has partnered with AI cloud companies to “deploy large‑scale, multi‑tenant AI factories” through a revenue-sharing and credit-support model, according to a statement on its website.
  • Through this new model, AI cloud companies will sell cloud services delivered through Nvidia DSX AI factories that manufacture tokens at scale.
  • “This accelerates the adoption of Nvidia platforms among customers, while giving these clouds a capital‑efficient path to scale and providing Nvidia with a new recurring, usage‑linked earnings stream,” the tech giant said in the statement.
  • Sharon AI (SHAZ) and Firmus are among the first companies to work with Nvidia on this strategy. Sharon AI is deploying up to 40,000 NVIDIA Grace Blackwell GB300 GPUs.
  • For model builders, inference providers, agent platforms, and enterprises scaling AI, it can mean faster access to full-stack accelerated computing without waiting through site selection, power procurement, construction, and hardware bring-up.

data.title: Top tech analyst Dan Ives exits Wedbush for new financial venture
data.description:
Renowned tech analyst Dan Ives is leaving Wedbush to launch an all-in-one merchant bank that combines research, advisory, capital raising, and investing.

“It’s been a phenomenal eight years at Wedbush,” Ives said in an interview with CNBC. “But in looking at the next opportunity, it’s to create a modern merchant bank with great partners, long-term capital, and something I think will change the way Wall Street looks at investment banks.”

The firm, which Ives said will be formally announced in the coming weeks, plans to provide proprietary research, strategic advisory services, capital raising, and investments across sectors including technology, energy, and financials. Ives said he also intends to continue covering technology stocks in a research capacity while helping build the broader business.

This transition represents a rare move for one of Wall Street's most prominent sell-side analysts, widely recognized by investors for his energetic TV appearances and highly bullish commentary on AI stocks.

Wedbush Fund Advisers will continue managing the IVES and IVEP ETFs, ensuring client continuity, it said in a statement.

Ives, a notable Tesla (TSLA) bull who views the company as a "physical AI" and autonomous technology powerhouse rather than a traditional automaker, on Tuesday initiated coverage on SpaceX (SPCX) with an Outperform rating and a $190 price target. Ives noted that SpaceX is well-positioned to evolve into a major hyperscaler.

He is best known for his high-profile, "perma-bull" coverage of major tech giants, most notably tracking Apple (AAPL), Tesla (TSLA), Microsoft (MSFT), and Nvidia (NVDA).

data.title: Thursday’s Economic Calendar
data.description:
Motor Vehicle Sales

Unit sales of motor vehicles, published by the Bureau of Economic Analysis at the beginning of each month, include domestic sales and imports.

Another flat reading is expected with sales at an annual 16.0 million in June after 16.1 million in May.

SIFMA Early Close 

7:45 AM Mary Daly Speaks

San Francisco Federal Reserve Bank President Mary Daly participates in "High-Level Policy Dialogue" before the 5th Banco de España-CEMFI-UIMP Conference on the Spanish Economy "Housing and Institutions: Challenges and Opportunities for Spain" conference, hosted by the Bank of Spain.

8:30 AM Employment Situation

The most closely watched of all economic indicators, the employment situation is a set of monthly labor market indicators based on two separate reports: the establishment survey, which tracks 650,000 worksites and offers the nonfarm payroll and average hourly earnings headlines, and the household survey, which interviews 60,000 households and generates the unemployment rate.

A moderate 114K increase in payrolls is the call with the jobless rate flat at 4.3%.

8:30 AM Jobless Claims 

New unemployment claims are compiled weekly to show the number of individuals who filed for unemployment insurance for the first time.

Claims appear stuck in this range of forecasts of 210K to 225K, with the consensus looking for 220K this week versus 215K last week.

10:00 AM Factory Orders

Factory orders represent the dollar level of new orders for both durable and nondurable goods.

After durable goods orders were reported down 4.5 percent for May on a drop in aircraft orders, the call for factory orders is down 2.0 percent on the month.

10:30 AM EIA Natural Gas Report 

The Energy Information Administration, or EIA, provides weekly information on natural gas stocks in underground storage for the U.S. and five regions of the country.

1:00 PM Baker Hughes Rig Count 

The Baker Hughes North American rig count tracks weekly changes in the number of active operating oil & gas rigs.

4:30 PM Fed Balance Sheet

The Fed's balance sheet is a weekly report presenting a consolidated balance sheet for all 12 Reserve Banks that lists factors supplying reserves into the banking system and factors absorbing reserves from the system.

data.title: Franklin Covey forecasts FY2026 revenue of $260M-$267M while maintaining adjusted EBITDA of $28M-$31M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Franklin Covey (FC) Q3 2026

Management view

  • “We are revising our revenue guidance to allow for a timing shift in $2 million of previously invoiced services for which the delivery shifted from this year to next year… a $2 million new school contract… that received gubernatorial budget reductions… and the approximately $2 million impact at the challenging international environment due to ongoing geopolitical tensions.” (President, CEO & Director Paul Walker)

  • “Our new expectation is that revenue will be between $260 million and $267 million. We are maintaining our prior adjusted EBITDA guidance within a narrower range of $28 million to $31 million.” (President, CEO & Director Walker)

  • “Total third quarter reported revenue was $67.8 million… Gross margin for the third quarter was 73.9%… Operating, selling, general and administrative expenses for the third quarter was $41.8 million… Adjusted EBITDA for the third quarter was $8.3 million.” (Executive VP & CFO Jessica Betjemann)

  • “Having completed our go-to-market transformation in Enterprise North America… we’re now importing those learnings into our international business.” (President, CEO & Director Walker)

Outlook

  • “Our new expectation is that revenue will be between $260 million and $267 million.” (President, CEO & Director Walker)

  • “We are maintaining our prior adjusted EBITDA guidance within a narrower range of $28 million to $31 million.” (President, CEO & Director Walker)

  • Management tied the revised revenue range to three items: delivery timing shifting into next year for an Enterprise North America contract, a state budget reduction affecting Education new-school funding expected to return next year, and weaker Enterprise International conditions tied to geopolitical tensions. (President, CEO & Director Walker; Executive VP & CFO Betjemann)

Financial results

  • The company reported Q3 revenue of $67.8 million and said revenue grew 1% over the prior year, with Enterprise and Education each up 2%. (Executive VP & CFO Betjemann)

  • The company reported gross margin of 73.9% (down from 76.5% a year ago) and attributed the decline primarily to increased delivery costs for services, mix, and increased capitalized curriculum amortization. (Executive VP & CFO Betjemann)

  • The company reported net income of $3.1 million (vs. a net loss of $1.4 million a year ago) and said restructuring expense was $0.7 million in Q3. (Executive VP & CFO Betjemann)

  • The company reported deferred revenue of $96 million (up 7% year-over-year), and noted Enterprise North America billed deferred revenue of $58 million (up 18% year-over-year). (President, CEO & Director Walker; Executive VP & CFO Betjemann)

Q&A

  • Alexander Paris, Barrington Research: Asked whether the revenue headwinds were macro-driven and requested underlying strength by business; President, CEO & Director Walker said the Enterprise North America issue was “just the timing of the delivery of that one contract,” Education was “isolated to 2 contracts,” and the main environmental pressure was international, including “weakness in our direct office operations in China” and impacts tied to the Middle East and Iran.

  • Alexander Paris, Barrington Research: Asked for an Education update into a seasonally important Q4; President of Education Division Michael Covey said retention was “running right now 1% to 2% higher than last year,” new-school growth without the delayed state deal would be “comparable to last year,” and cited momentum in “charter schools and after schools” as adjacent markets.

  • Alexander Paris, Barrington Research: Asked about Enterprise retention/new logos and government; President of Enterprise Division Holly Procter cited “both strong retention and strong expansion,” and said, “We have not yet seen our government business have an uptick.”

  • David Joseph Storms, Stonegate: Asked about extending the go-to-market model internationally; President Procter said Europe is first, shifting to a “hunter-farmer structure,” with “a go-live date in Q1” and rollout “over next year.”

  • David Joseph Storms, Stonegate: Pressed on China; President, CEO & Director Walker said management was “looking at some options” and would share more after evaluating “different options” for operating China.

  • Nehal Chokshi, Northland: Asked what best indicates underlying strength; Executive VP & CFO Betjemann pointed to additions in “subscription and contractually committed invoiced amounts,” saying it “translate[s] into the net revenue growth into next year.”

  • Jeff Martin, ROTH: Asked about sales productivity and whether the environment is choppy; President Procter said per-person productivity is up versus the prior model, citing added support functions such as an SDR team that helps ramp new hires faster.

  • Alexander Paris, Barrington Research: Asked for the AI roadmap; President, CEO & Director Walker said Franklin Covey had launched AI offerings, expanded work with a large technology client in Q3, and planned “the next set of modules” in early fall plus “additional functionality within our AI coach.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts: Slightly negative to neutral, with questions repeatedly probing whether weakness reflected macro pressure and whether international/China issues are structural, including “Is there any thoughts around what could put that back on track…?” (David Joseph Storms, Stonegate)

  • Management: Slightly positive in prepared remarks and neutral in Q&A, emphasizing visibility and timing items; President, CEO & Director Walker said, “We’re not seeing really Enterprise North America a change right now on the larger environment,” and Executive VP & CFO Betjemann highlighted that invoiced growth is expected to “translate to net revenue growth into next year.”

  • Versus last quarter: Management language shifted from reaffirming guidance to revising revenue guidance while reiterating fiscal ’27 expectations; analysts remained focused on macro, but Q3 added sharper emphasis on international/geopolitical and China execution questions.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance changed: Q2 guidance was “$265 million to $275 million” revenue and adjusted EBITDA “$28 million to $33 million,” while Q3 moved revenue to $260 million-$267 million and narrowed adjusted EBITDA to $28 million-$31 million. (Executive VP & CFO Betjemann)

  • International narrative changed: Q2 described International invoiced momentum and direct-office strength; Q3 highlighted weaker Enterprise International revenue, citing China and geopolitical impacts, alongside a plan to deploy the Enterprise North America go-to-market playbook into Europe. (President, CEO & Director Walker; President Procter)

  • Education narrative changed: Q2 emphasized strong Education momentum and expected a back-half seasonal lift; Q3 centered on a state funding cut delaying new-school launches, with management maintaining that it did “not reflect… weakness in school and district demand for Leader in Me.” (President, CEO & Director Walker)

Risks and concerns

  • Management cited risks from “ongoing geopolitical tensions,” including direct impacts to Enterprise International and uncertainty in China operations. (President, CEO & Director Walker)

  • Management flagged execution risk around delivery timing of contracted services and variability in the fourth-quarter close, while emphasizing cost reductions as the mitigation supporting adjusted EBITDA guidance. (President, CEO & Director Walker; Executive VP & CFO Betjemann)

Final takeaway

Management framed fiscal 2026 as a year where invoiced growth and deferred revenue build a base for fiscal 2027, but revised the full-year revenue range to $260 million-$267 million due to service-delivery timing shifts, an Education state funding cut, and international/geopolitical pressures. Management maintained adjusted EBITDA guidance at $28 million-$31 million, pointed to cost reductions and restructuring actions, and emphasized Enterprise North America momentum, a plan to export the go-to-market model into Europe, and continued AI solution launches and client demand.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Greenbrier forecasts $3-$3.15 FY2026 EPS while expanding lease fleet growth strategy
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The Greenbrier Companies (GBX) Q3 fiscal 2026

Management View

  • "Greenbrier delivered solid commercial, operational and financial results in the third quarter." (President, CEO & Director Lorie Leeson)
  • "The current industry forecasts indicate less than 25,000 new railcars for calendar 2026, which will be the lowest level recorded since 2010." (President, CEO & Director Leeson)
  • "We remain focused on growing this platform and doubling our recurring revenue base by 2028 through both our own manufacturing operations and secondary market opportunities as they arise." (President, CEO & Director Leeson)
  • "We received orders for 2,200 railcars during the quarter, valued at $340 million." (Executive Vice President & President of The Americas Brian Comstock)
  • "We ended the quarter with 13,800 railcars valued at $2 billion." (Executive Vice President & President of The Americas Comstock)
  • "We expanded the owned lease fleet to 20,600 railcars and utilization remained exceptionally strong at 99%." (Executive Vice President & President of The Americas Comstock)
  • "Total revenue for the quarter was $577 million." (Senior VP & CFO Michael Donfris)

Outlook

  • "For fiscal 2026, we continue to expect total revenue of $2.4 billion to $2.5 billion and are narrowing our expected EPS range to $3 to $3.15 per share." (Senior VP & CFO Donfris)
  • "Our fiscal 2026 outlook is based on our latest view of fourth quarter manufacturing margins and delivery timing, reflecting that some activity is moving into fiscal 2027." (Senior VP & CFO Donfris)
  • The analysts estimates tag provided in the prompt does not match the required format described in the instructions (it uses numeric fiscal_quarter fields rather than Q1-Q4), so no estimates comparison is included.

Financial Results

  • "Leasing & Fleet Management revenue was $47 million, up 3% from Q2." (Senior VP & CFO Donfris)
  • "Manufacturing revenue was $529 million, down about 2% sequentially." (Senior VP & CFO Donfris)
  • "Aggregate gross margin was 14.1%, within our long-term target range and improved from Q2." (Senior VP & CFO Donfris)
  • "Diluted earnings per share were $0.60 and EBITDA was $69 million or about 12% of revenue." (Senior VP & CFO Donfris)
  • "We ended the quarter with total liquidity of approximately $887 million, representing $274 million in cash and $613 million of available borrowing capacity." (Senior VP & CFO Donfris)
  • "Operating cash flow for the quarter reflects $227 million of investment, primarily for leased railcars purchased in the secondary market." (Senior VP & CFO Donfris)
  • "We also refinanced our leasing term loan with a new $300 million facility, extending the maturity by 6 years, improving credit terms and adding a delayed draw that provides up to $125 million of additional capacity to support future growth." (Senior VP & CFO Donfris)

Q&A

  • Andrzej Tomczyk, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division: asked about potential Section 232 tariffs on tank cars from Mexico and any current payments; President, CEO & Director Leeson replied, "We are not currently entering tank cars or paying a tariff for equipment that's coming from Mexico into the United States," and added, "we and our industry partners are seeking guidance from CBP on how best to navigate that."
  • Andrzej Tomczyk, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division: asked about backlog tank-car mix, retroactive tariff risk, and pass-through; President, CEO & Director Leeson said, "we believe that any adjustments associated with tariffs would be passed through to our customers," called retroactivity "unclear," and stated, "about 20% of that is tank cars"; Executive Vice President & President of The Americas Comstock added the tank-car mix is "quickly diminishing" and that Greenbrier has "provisions in all of our contracts to pass through tariffs and duties as appropriate."
  • Andrzej Tomczyk, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division: asked about shifting tank-car production to Arkansas; President, CEO & Director Leeson responded, "we're building tank cars there right now, and we are evaluating how much we could shift," citing workforce availability; Executive Vice President & President of The Americas Comstock said, "we have the capability to take on quite a bit of that capacity if need be."
  • Harrison Bauer, Susquehanna: asked whether tariffs/couplers/EPA case are restraining demand; President, CEO & Director Leeson said, "it's really not" and pointed instead to "the broader macroeconomic situation"; Executive Vice President & President of The Americas Comstock added tariffs "do not apply to cars that are being moved into Canada" and said the company is seeing a "shift" toward "more covered hopper cars, black cars, special purpose assets and really higher value backlog."
  • Harrison Bauer, Susquehanna: asked for an update on the coupler case and sourcing options; President, CEO & Director Leeson said, "today, we actually filed our administrative appeal" and described the determination as having "industry-wide implications"; Executive Vice President & President of The Americas Comstock said, "the financial impact of the couplers is fairly small on a per unit basis" and "it's probably less than 1% of the total impact."
  • Adam Roszkowski, BofA Securities, Research Division: asked why FY2026 revenue guidance was unchanged while deliveries/margins/EPS cadence shifted toward 2027; Senior VP & CFO Donfris said, "we are seeing a little bit move into 2027" and cited "a little bit on absorption," adding, "we're just getting much more closer to being able to call the year."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly negative to pressing, centered on policy and regulatory uncertainty and its commercial impact, including "recent amendments to Section 232 investigations" and questions on "retroactive payments" and whether customers are "waiting for some clarity" (Andrzej Tomczyk, Goldman Sachs; Harrison Bauer, Susquehanna).
  • Management sentiment was slightly positive and compliance-focused on tariffs/couplers, emphasizing current status and process steps, including "We are not currently entering tank cars or paying a tariff" and "today, we actually filed our administrative appeal" (President, CEO & Director Leeson).
  • Compared with Q2, the Q3 exchange placed more emphasis on trade/regulatory mechanics (Section 232 and CBP rulings) versus Q2’s heavier focus on production timing shifts and European footprint changes; management in both quarters used similar confidence language on demand timing, including "it's a matter of when, not if" (President, CEO & Director Leeson, Q3).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • FY2026 guidance language tightened in Q3: "narrowing our expected EPS range to $3 to $3.15 per share" (Senior VP & CFO Donfris) versus Q2: "forecasting EPS between $3 and $3.50 per share" (Senior VP & CFO Donfris).
  • The lease-fleet strategy stayed central, but Q3 highlighted a step-change in owned fleet and secondary buying: "expanded the owned lease fleet to 20,600 railcars" and "secondary market acquisitions of approximately 4,400 railcars" (Executive Vice President & President of The Americas Comstock) versus Q2’s expectation to "finish fiscal 2026 with a lease fleet of over 20,000 railcars" (Executive Vice President & President of The Americas Comstock).
  • Analyst focus shifted from Q2 concerns about backlog/cycle and secondary-market gains to Q3’s concentration on tariffs/couplers and pass-through mechanics.

Risks and Concerns

  • "There has been some recent pronouncements and determinations that are -- that have industry-wide implications" on tariffs, and management said it is "seeking guidance from CBP on how best to navigate that." (President, CEO & Director Leeson)
  • On tariff exposure, management said retroactivity is uncertain: "when it comes to retroactive obligations, right now, that's unclear." (President, CEO & Director Leeson)
  • On the coupler matter, management framed it as broader than Greenbrier and said, "the CBP's determination letter does have industry-wide implications," while also stating the per-unit impact is "probably less than 1% of the total impact." (President, CEO & Director Leeson; Executive Vice President & President of The Americas Comstock)

Final Takeaway

Management described Q3 as sequentially stronger on margin and earnings, while reiterating that macro conditions support lease rates and utilization but continue to pressure newbuild demand. The company emphasized lease-fleet expansion and secondary-market buying to grow recurring revenue, maintained FY2026 revenue guidance at $2.4 billion to $2.5 billion, and narrowed FY2026 EPS guidance to $3 to $3.15 per share while noting some activity is shifting into fiscal 2027. In Q&A, investors’ biggest swing factor was potential Section 232/CBP tariff interpretation for Mexico-built tank cars and the CBP coupler ruling, with management stating it is not currently paying tariffs, expects pass-through to customers, and has filed an administrative appeal.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Oil flows through Strait of Hormuz reportedly surge past 10M bbl/day
data.description:
Commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz has surged in recent weeks, with American military support helping boost oil flows to more than 10M bbl/day, Bloomberg reported Wednesday, citing an unnamed U.S. official.

The increase since President Trump signed the memorandum of understanding represents a significant jump in traffic that has caught Iran off guard, indicating its now-limited ability to halt traffic through the strait, according to the official cited in the report.

Even before the MoU, the U.S. took steps to weaken Iran's control of the strait, as military support including naval and air forces coordinated by U.S. Central Command reportedly has raised the confidence of shippers to move oil through the southern portion of the waterway closer to Oman.

Iran's limited capacity to monitor traffic farther from its coastline has dimmed its awareness of activity in the southern transit corridor, leading to a relatively late realization of the extent of the region's oil flows, the official told Bloomberg.

Crude oil futures fell Wednesday to their lowest levels since before the February 28 start of the U.S.-Iran conflict, as optimism over negotiations eased supply concerns after President Trump said the meetings in Qatar had gone well.

"The negotiations... are ​perceived as being positive [and] that has allowed prices to drift further," Saxo Bank analyst Ole Hansen said in a note, adding that "there is a ​chance that we could see even lower prices."

U.S. crude oil inventories fell for a 10th straight week in the week ended June 26, the U.S. Energy Information Administration reported.

Commercial crude oil stocks excluding the Strategic Petroleum Reserve, fell by a larger-than-expected 3.8M barrels to 408.4M barrels and were ~7% below the five-year average for the time of year, the EIA said.

U.S. crude oil production stayed flat at 13.8M bbl/day, crude imports fell by 291K to 5.3M bbl/day, and exports fell by 661K to 4M bbl/day; U.S. refineries ran at 96.6% of capacity, up from 96.1% in the previous week.

Gasoline inventories fell by 2.3M barrels to 214M barrels and were 7% below the five-year average, and distillate fuel stocks rose by 2.5M barrels to 108.6M barrels and were 8% below the five-year average for the time of year, according to the EIA.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for August delivery dropped 1.3% to $68.58/bbl, and front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for September delivery slipped 1.9% to $71.57/bbl, the lowest for both oil benchmarks since late February, while front-month Nymex natural gas (NG1:COM) for August delivery fell 1.7% to $3.220/MMBtu.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)
